title: Concussion / Mild Traumatic Brain Injury
symptoms: Headache or a feeling of pressure in the head
Temporary confusion or feeling in a fog
immediate_steps: Have the person stop the activity and rest
Apply a cold compress to any swelling on the head
Monitor them continuously for worsening symptoms
Seek medical evaluation to confirm the diagnosis and rule out severe injury
Ensure they rest completely (physical and mental rest)
avoid: Do not let them return to sports or rigorous activity
Do not give them aspirin or ibuprofen if severe bleeding inside the skull is suspected (use acetaminophen if directed by a doctor)

title: Severe Head Injury / Skull Fracture
symptoms: Clear fluid or blood leaking from the nose or ears
Weakness or numbness in the body
immediate_steps: Keep the person completely still; assume there is a spinal injury
Stop any bleeding by applying firm pressure with a sterile cloth (unless you suspect a skull fracture, then apply gentle pressure around the wound)
Monitor breathing and be prepared for CPR
If they vomit, roll their entire body to the side (log-roll) to prevent choking
avoid: Do not move the person unless absolutely necessary
Do not apply direct pressure to a bleeding head wound if you suspect the skull is fractured
Do not remove any penetrating objects
Do not wash a deep head wound
